topic: Deep Learning: CNNs for Image Recognition & RNNs for Sequence Data

questions.scenario: An autonomous drone inspection startup is deploying a real-time crack detection system on edge devices to monitor concrete bridge health. The camera captures high-resolution images, but the latency budget is strictly under 50 milliseconds per frame. The initial PyTorch Mobile model uses a standard ResNet-50 backbone, which achieves high accuracy but takes 450 milliseconds per frame. Additionally, the training dataset is highly skewed: 98% of the images contain no cracks, while only 2% contain concrete defects.
questions.question: Which architecture modification and loss function pair should the lead AI engineer choose to satisfy the strict real-time latency constraints on edge hardware while preventing the model from ignoring the minority class?
options: A) Replace ResNet-50 with MobileNetV3-Large using Depthwise Separable Convolutions, and replace Cross-Entropy Loss with Focal Loss with gamma=2.
B) Retain ResNet-50 but apply 8-bit post-training quantization (PTQ) to accelerate inference, and use weighted Cross-Entropy Loss with class weights inversely proportional to class frequencies.
C) Replace ResNet-50 with a standard VGG-16 backbone to reduce model depth, and utilize Dice Loss to handle the spatial segmentation of the thin cracks.
D) Transition to an Encoder-Decoder UNet with Group Normalization, and optimize the system using Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss on downsampled 128x128 input images.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) Replace ResNet-50 with MobileNetV3-Large using Depthwise Separable Convolutions, and replace Cross-Entropy Loss with Focal Loss with gamma=2.
questions.explanation: MobileNetV3 uses depthwise separable convolutions which drastically reduce the number of Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) operations, allowing the model to fit within the 50ms edge computing latency budget where ResNet-50 fails. For the extreme 98/2 class imbalance, standard Cross-Entropy loss is dominated by the easy-to-classify background examples. Focal Loss solves this by adding a modulating factor (1 - p)^gamma to the loss, dynamically down-weighting the loss contribution of easy examples during training and forcing the model to focus on the rare crack features.

questions.scenario: A financial technology company is building an automated algorithmic trading agent that processes tick-by-tick transaction logs of highly volatile stock indexes. The input sequence length is extremely long, exceeding 5,000 timesteps per training window, to capture macro-level daily trends. The engineering team deployed a standard Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. During training, the GPU VRAM is exhausted instantly. When the batch size is reduced to 1, training stability collapses, and the model completely fails to capture dependencies spanning across the early phases of the sequence.
questions.question: What architectural and training strategy transition is most appropriate to resolve the VRAM bottleneck while ensuring the network successfully learns long-range dependencies across the entire sequence?
options: A) Replace the LSTM with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) to reduce the parameter count by 33%, and apply standard Gradient Clipping with a threshold of 1.0.

B) Replace the LSTM with a Transformer model utilizing Linear Attention (such as Performer or Linformer) to scale computational complexity to O(T) instead of O(T^2), and train using mixed-precision FP16.
C) Retain the LSTM architecture but implement Truncated Backpropagation Through Time (TBPTT) with a maximum backward window limit of 100 timesteps.
D) Keep the LSTM and apply Tensor-Train decomposition to the recurrent weight matrices to compress model memory footprint, coupled with a learning rate warm-up schedule.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Replace the LSTM with a Transformer model utilizing Linear Attention (such as Performer or Linformer) to scale computational complexity to O(T) instead of O(T^2), and train using mixed-precision FP16.
questions.explanation: For sequence lengths (T) greater than 5,000, standard LSTMs suffer from both memory bottlenecks and sequential dependency limitations. Standard Transformers scale quadratically O(T^2) with respect to sequence length, which would worsen VRAM usage. However, Linear Attention Transformers reduce this complexity to O(T), making very long sequences computationally feasible. Mixed-precision (FP16) training further halves the VRAM footprint. While Truncated BPTT (Option C) would solve the memory issue, it limits the gradient flow to 100 timesteps, making it impossible to capture long-range dependencies spanning the 5,000 timesteps.

questions.scenario: A smart warehouse relies on automated guided vehicles (AGVs) for inventory transport. You are tasked with developing a video-based collision avoidance system that detects if a worker is walking into an AGV's path within the next 2 seconds (sequence length of 60 frames at 30 FPS). The prototype architecture extracts spatial features from each frame using a frozen CNN backbone, then feeds these temporal feature vectors into a Bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM). Although this model achieves 96% accuracy during offline validation, during real-time deployment the AGVs repeatedly crash into obstacles because the system introduces massive latency.
questions.question: What is the primary cause of this real-time failure, and how should the architecture be re-engineered to resolve it while maintaining temporal modeling accuracy?
options: A) The CNN feature maps are too high-dimensional; apply Global Average Pooling (GAP) before feeding them into the Bi-LSTM to reduce spatial dimensionality.

B) The Bi-LSTM is inherently non-causal because the backward pass requires future frames (up to t+60) to compute hidden states; replace it with a Unidirectional LSTM combined with an attention layer, and feed temporal difference frames into the CNN.
C) The frozen CNN weights prevent gradient flow during temporal training; unfreeze the final three convolutional layers of the CNN to perform end-to-end backpropagation.
D) The 60-frame sequence exceeds the memory retention capability of the LSTM; replace the recurrent layers with a 3D Convolutional Network (3D-CNN) using non-overlapping temporal strides.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The Bi-LSTM is inherently non-causal because the backward pass requires future frames (up to t+60) to compute hidden states; replace it with a Unidirectional LSTM combined with an attention layer, and feed temporal difference frames into the CNN.
questions.explanation: A Bidirectional LSTM processes the sequence in both forward and backward directions. In a real-time deployment, calculating the backward state at time 't' requires access to future frames (up to 't+N'), creating an artificial delay of 2 seconds (60 frames) before an inference can be made, leading to collisions. Replacing it with a Unidirectional LSTM ensures strict temporal causality (only past/present frames are used). To compensate for the loss of future-context accuracy, adding an attention mechanism over past states highlights critical actions, and feeding temporal difference frames (I_t - I_t-1) provides explicit motion cues directly to the spatial CNN.
